global.title: SNO Components and Legal Foundations

coreIdeas.contextPhrase: The framing claim — SNOs exist because DAOs failed at scaling; they combine decentralized principles with operational rigor, providing the structured framework that DAOs lacked
The structural overview — five entities each fulfill a critical role: DAO (governance), OH (admin/compliance), OCF (investment/POWTs), RGH (commercial revenue), IPSPV (IP protection)
The compliance solution — the OH uses Swiss Associations to handle contributor relationships, tax reporting, cross-border compliance, and bank interactions, shielding contributors from liability
The IP strategy — dual licensing lets the IPSPV support open-source availability via copyleft while generating enterprise revenue from proprietary licenses, funding the ecosystem
The governance tool — MPAs define multisig signer roles and responsibilities, reducing the chaos of decentralized decision-making while protecting signers from inadvertent legal exposure

The early-stage wrapper — Swiss Associations don't require registration, preserve privacy, and operate within Switzerland's crypto-friendly regulatory environment, ideal for DAOs transitioning to SNOs
The friction reducer — standardized contributor agreements, MPAs, and licensing contracts let SNOs focus on innovation instead of navigating legal complexity from scratch
The first live product — turnkey legal and operational setup as a subscription service with concrete pricing (50-1250/mo)
Concrete pricing data — Starter at 50/mo, Standard at ,250/mo + 50/contributor, representing Powerhouse's first recurring revenue model
The document model that codifies operational hub configurations and compliance settings
Swiss associations provide non-commercial, privacy-preserving legal wrappers for SNOs

global.description: Scalable Network Organizations: five entities (DAO, OH, OCF, RGH, IPSPV) plus legal tools (MPAs, Swiss Associations, OSS templates).
global.orientation: Scalable Network Organizations are Powerhouse's answer to the DAO's broken promises. Where DAOs struggled with legal ambiguity, resource allocation, and incentive misalignment, SNOs provide a structured five-entity framework: the DAO for on-chain governance and strategic vision, the Operational Hub for contributor management and compliance via Swiss Associations, the OCF for investment and POWT issuance, the RGH for commercial revenue (licensing, SaaS, consulting), and the IPSPV for IP protection with dual licensing. This structure is backstopped by legal innovations — Multisig Participation Agreements that formalize signer accountability, Swiss Associations that provide privacy-preserving legal wrappers, and open-source legal templates that reduce setup costs. Together these components let decentralized organizations operate with the rigor of traditional institutions.
